full_response:
Let  

• CLIQUE = { ⟨G=(V ,E), k⟩ | G contains a clique of size ≥ k }  
• HAPPY-FRIEND = { ⟨S₁,…,S_n ,h⟩ | it is possible to give the gifts 1,…,m so that ≥ h friends i receive exactly the set S_i }  

We show

1. HAPPY-FRIEND ∈ NP, and  
2. CLIQUE ≤_P HAPPY-FRIEND.

Together these give NP-completeness.


────────────────────────────────────────
1 HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP
────────────────────────────────────────
A certificate is  
• a set H ⊆ {1,…,n}, |H| = h, of friends we claim to make happy, and  
• a partition of the gifts among H ( friend i ∈ H gets exactly S_i ).

The verifier checks in O(∑_{i∈H}|S_i|) ≤ O(n·m) time that  
(a) the assigned set for every i ∈ H equals S_i, and  
(b) the S_i ’s are pairwise disjoint (no gift is reused).  
Thus HAPPY-FRIEND ∈ NP.



────────────────────────────────────────
2 Polynomial reduction CLIQUE → HAPPY-FRIEND
────────────────────────────────────────
Input to CLIQUE: a simple graph G=(V ,E) with |V| = n and an integer k.

Construction (performed in O(n²) time).

Gifts.  
For every unordered pair {u,v} of distinct vertices that is NOT an edge of G
  create one gift g_{uv}.  
There are at most 𝚖 =  ( n choose 2 ) − |E|  ≤ n² gifts.

Friends and their demands.  
Create n friends, one per vertex.  
For vertex v let

  S_v = { g_{uv} | u≠v and {u,v} ∉ E }.

That is, S_v contains exactly the gifts that correspond to the non-edges incident to v.

Set h := k.

This produces the HAPPY-FRIEND instance ⟨ S_1,…,S_n , h = k ⟩.



────────────────────────────────────────
3 Correctness of the reduction
────────────────────────────────────────
We prove

 G has a clique of size k  ⇔  at least h = k friends can be made happy.

Lemma 1.  
For distinct vertices p,q ∈ V, the sets S_p and S_q overlap
  ⇔ {p,q} is NOT an edge of G.

Proof.  By construction g_{pq} ∈ S_p∩S_q exactly when {p,q} ∉ E; no other gift is shared by the two friends. ∎


(⇒) Suppose C ⊆ V is a clique, |C| = k.  
By Lemma 1, any two vertices of C are adjacent, hence S_p ∩ S_q = ∅ for p≠q in C.
Therefore the k sets {S_v | v∈C} are pairwise disjoint; we can give each friend v∈C the gifts S_v, making all k friends happy.

(⇐) Conversely, assume we can make k friends simultaneously happy.  
Because a gift cannot be given twice, the k corresponding sets must be pairwise disjoint.
Hence, again by Lemma 1, no two of those k vertices miss an edge; therefore every pair is adjacent.  The k vertices thus induce a clique of size k in G.

Thus the CLIQUE instance is YES iff the produced HAPPY-FRIEND instance is YES.



────────────────────────────────────────
4 Complexity of the reduction
────────────────────────────────────────
The construction examines each unordered pair of vertices once, taking Θ(n²) time and space—polynomial in the size of ⟨G,k⟩.



────────────────────────────────────────
5 Conclusion
────────────────────────────────────────
HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP, and CLIQUE ≤_P HAPPY-FRIEND.  
Since CLIQUE is NP-complete, HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-complete.

\boxed{\text{HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-complete by a polynomial reduction from CLIQUE}}  

Confidence: 0.31
extracted_answer: \text{HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-complete by a polynomial reduction from CLIQUE}
